---
title: "HW #4.3: Reproducible Data Analysis"
author: "Shlok Channawar"
date: today
format: pdf
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

#loads required libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(scales)
```

# Busiest Airports Analysis

## Introduction

In this section we will analyze passenger traffic trends across six of the busiest airports 
in the world from 2020 to 2025. Our goal is to compare how different airports were affected 
by the COVID-19 pandemic and identify patterns in decline and recovery.

## Data and Visualization

```{r}
#| label: airports-data

airports <- tibble(
  airport = c(
    rep("Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International", 6),
    rep("Dubai International Airport", 6),
    rep("Tokyo Haneda Airport", 6),
    rep("Heathrow Airport", 6),
    rep("Frankfurt Airport", 6),
    rep("Beijing Daxing International Airport", 6)
  ),
  code = c(
    rep("ATL", 6), rep("DXB", 6), rep("HND", 6),
    rep("LHR", 6), rep("FRA", 6), rep("PKX", 6)
  ),
  year = as.integer(rep(2020:2025, 6)),
  passengers = c(
    42918685, 75704760, 93699630, 104653451, 108067766, 106302208,
    25836771, 29110609, 66069981, 86994365, 92331506, 95200000,
    31055210, 25876429, 50290705, 78719302, 85900167, 91430000,
    22111469, 19395000, 61614508, 79183364, 83884572, 84463000,
    18768601, 24812849, 48918482, 59355389, 61564957, 63189666,
    16090000, 25051012, 36361000, 39414905, 49441029, 53618949
  )
)
```

```{r}
#| label: airports-table

airports |>
  mutate(passengers = format(passengers, big.mark = ",")) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = passengers) |>
  select(-airport) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Code", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024", "2025"),
    align = "lrrrrrr",
    caption = "Annual Passenger Traffic for Six Major Airports (2020-2025)"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-airports
#| fig-cap: "Annual passenger traffic for six major airports from 2020 to 2025."
#| fig-alt: "Line chart showing annual passenger traffic for six major airports from 2020 to 2025."

# Line plot showing passenger trends over time coloured by airport code
ggplot(airports, aes(x = year, y = passengers, color = code, group = code)) +  
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2020:2025) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Annual Passenger Traffic for Six Major Airports 2020-25",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Passengers",
    color = "Airport Code"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

COVID-19 devastated passenger traffic in 2020 across all six airports. ATL, normally the 
world's busiest, dropped to just 42.9 million passengers, while LHR and FRA fell below 
23 million. PKX had only opened in 2019 and recorded just 16 million in its first full 
pandemic year. The plot shows a steep recovery from 2021 to 2023 as travel restrictions 
lifted, with most airports nearly doubling traffic year over year. ATL maintained its top 
position throughout, peaking at 108 million in 2024. A notable pattern visible in the plot 
but easy to miss in the table is LHR dropping below PKX and FRA in 2021 before recovering 
strongly. By 2024-2025, traffic leveled off for most airports, though PKX continued growing 
every single year without exception and has not yet plateaued.






# Monte Carlo Numerical Integration

## Introduction

This section uses Monte Carlo numerical integration to estimate the area under the 
probability density function of the standard normal distribution over the interval 
$-4 \leq x \leq 4$, with $0 \leq y \leq 0.4$. The simulation is run at four resolutions 
(n = 10, 100, 1000, 10000) to show how accuracy improves with more points.

## Simulation and Visualization

```{r}
#| label: monte-carlo-setup

# Generate n random (x, y) coordinate pairs within specified bounds
monte_carlo_points <- function(n, x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max) { 
  tibble(
    x = runif(n, min = x_min, max = x_max),
    y = runif(n, min = y_min, max = y_max)
  )
}

# run simulation at a given resolution and thren return a ggplot
make_mc_plot <- function(n) {
  sim_data <- monte_carlo_points(n = n, x_min = -4, x_max = 4, y_min = 0, y_max = 0.4) |>
    mutate(flag = ifelse(y <= dnorm(x, mean = 0, sd = 1), "on/below", "above"))

  est_integration <- mean(sim_data$flag == "on/below") * 3.2    #Estimate integral = proportion of points on/below curve * area of bounding box

  ggplot(sim_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = flag)) +
    geom_point(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.6) +
    stat_function(
      fun = dnorm,
      args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1),
      xlim = c(-4, 4),
      color = "blue",
      linewidth = 1
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("above" = "red", "on/below" = "green")) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("MC Integration (n=", n, ")"),
      subtitle = paste("Est. Numerical Integration:", round(est_integration, 4)),
      x = "X",
      y = "Y",
      color = "flag"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()
}
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-montecarlo
#| fig-cap: "Small multiple showing Monte Carlo numerical integration of the standard normal distribution at four resolutions"
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "n = 10"
#|   - "n = 100"
#|   - "n = 1000"
#|   - "n = 10000"
#| layout-ncol: 2

make_mc_plot(10)
make_mc_plot(100)
make_mc_plot(1000)
make_mc_plot(10000)
```

The small multiple makes it clear that more points produce a better estimate. At n=10 the 
estimate is noticeably off since there are barely any points to work with. By n=10000 the 
estimate converges to approximately 1.0 , very close to the true value of 1. The true 
answer is 1 because the standard normal distribution is a probability density function, 
meaning the total area under the curve must equal 1 by definition. Integrating from -4 to 4 
captures nearly all of that area since values beyond 4 standard deviations are extremely 
rare. The n=10000 panel is the most convincing visually, with green points filling almost 
perfectly under the bell curve.




# Planning and Prompting GenAI Tools

## My Plan

**Goal:** Tidy the calcium dataset and create a visualization that helps explore differences 
in ulnar calcium measurements across time and between the two treatment groups (null vs. 
diet/exercise).

**Nouns:** calcium data, treatment group, time point, ulnar calcium measurement, tidy data 
frame, line plot, average, individual participant lines

**Verbs:** read, pivot, rename, mutate, group, summarize, plot, compare

**Steps:**

1. Read in the calcium.csv file using `read_csv()`
2. The data is not tidy - there are 8 columns total, the first four (Initial, Year 1, Year 2, Year 3) belong to the null treatment group and the last four belong to the diet/exercise group. Pivot each set of four columns separately to long format.
3. After pivoting, add a group column to each that labels rows as either "Null" or "Diet/Exercise", then bind the two together into one data frame.
4. Clean up the time variable so the values read as "Initial", "Year 1", "Year 2", "Year 3" in the correct order using a factor.
5. Calculate the average ulnar calcium measurement at each time point for each group.
6. Create a line plot with time on the x-axis and ulnar calcium on the y-axis, showing individual participant lines faintly in the background and a bold group average line on top, colored by treatment group.

## Plan-Informed Response

Using the plan above along with the calcium.csv file Claude (claude-sonnet-4-6) 
was prompted to write R code to tidy the data and produce a visualization. The LLM correctly 
identified the duplicate column name issue in the raw CSV and handled it by selecting the 
renamed columns (y0, y1, y2, y3 for null and y0.1, y1.1, y2.1, y3.1 for diet/exercise). It 
added participant IDs using row_number() before pivoting to preserve individual trajectories 
for plotting. The resulting plot shows individual participant lines faintly in the background 
with bold group average lines on top, colored by treatment group.

```{r}
#| label: fig-plan-informed
#| fig-cap: "Ulnar calcium measurements over time by treatment group, from plan-informed Claude response."
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"

knitr::include_graphics("/Users/shloki/Desktop/stat184/hw4.3/CalciumPlanPlot.png")
```

## Generic Response

The following is the output from Anthropic Claude (claude-sonnet-4-6) when given a generic 
prompt with no plan: *"Create a data visualization that will help me understand these data. 
Calcium.csv"*

```{r}
#| label: fig-generic-1
#| fig-cap: "Generic prompt response - group means view."
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"

knitr::include_graphics("/Users/shloki/Desktop/stat184/hw4.3/ClaudePlot1.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-generic-2
#| fig-cap: "Generic prompt response - individual lines view."
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"

knitr::include_graphics("/Users/shloki/Desktop/stat184/hw4.3/ClaudePlot2.png")
```

Claude also noted that both groups follow the same pattern, staying relatively stable across the 
first three time points before dropping noticeably at the final measurement. Group A started 
slightly higher at baseline (mean 72.4 vs. 69.3) but both groups converged to nearly 
identical final values (64.8 and 64.5). Claude also flagged substantial individual variation 
and noted it could not interpret the groups without knowing what they represented.

## Comparison

The plan-informed response produced cleaner amd more interpretable R code that directly 
addressed the context of the study. By specifying the treatment group labels (Null vs. 
Diet/Exercise), the correct time point names (Initial, Year 1, Year 2, Year 3) and the 
individual participant lines layered under group averages, the output was immediately usable 
and contextually accurate. The generic response, while visually polished, used generic 
labels like "Group A" and "Group B" and "Wave 1/Wave 2" which strip away the actual meaning 
of the study. It also produced an interactive widget rather than static R code, making it 
harder to reproduce or embed in a document. The key lesson is that a detailed plan 
dramatically reduces the amount of cleanup needed after the AI responds and produces output 
that is specific to the context rather than a generic template.

# Reflection

Coming into this course with some prior R experience, the biggest shift has been moving from 
writing code that just works to writing code that communicates properly. The approach of 
writing goals, nouns, verbs, and steps before touching any code was completely new to me. 
In the airports analysis for example, having a clear plan upfront meant I knew exactly that 
I needed both a wide-format table and a long-format plot, which are two different data 
structures from the same dataset. Writing a function that takes n as an input and returns a full 
plot and then calling it four times inside a Quarto layout, felt different from just writing a 
script top to bottom. It forced me to think about what changes between runs and what stays the same.

The GenAI section was also surprising in terms of how much the quality of a prompt matters. 
The plan-informed response from Claude was immediately usable with correct group labels and 
time point names, while the generic one used "Group A", "Group B", and "Wave 1/Wave 2" 
which required interpretation just to figure out what the groups even represented.
Looking back in the last 2-3 months the area I struggled with most was thinking about data structure before writing code. 
Early in the course I would jump straight into coding without a clear plan, which led to a lot of backtracking. 
If I were to redo assignments from the beginning, I would spend more time on the planning step and treat 
it as essential rather than optional busywork.

# Appendix A: GenAI Usage
## Planning and Prompting GenAI Tools Section

**Tool:** Anthropic Claude (claude-sonnet-4-6)  
**Date:** April 15, 2026

**Prompt 1:** hey so you will help me write R code for the calcium data set ill upload with this, ill provide nouns, verbs and goals with that. you then write the code and give it to me. if this sounds good lemme know or if u have any questions before that, please state but dont just do stuff without asking me okay?
**Response 1:** Sounds good! Go ahead and upload the dataset and share the nouns, verbs, and goals whenever you're ready. I'll wait before doing anything.

**Prompt 2:**Goal: Tidy the calcium dataset and create a visualization that helps explore differences in ulnar calcium measurements across time and between the two treatment groups (null vs. diet/exercise).
Nouns: calcium data, treatment group, time point, ulnar calcium measurement, tidy data frame, line plot, average, individual participant lines
Verbs: read, pivot, rename, mutate, group, summarize, plot, compare
Steps:
1) Read in the calcium.csv file using read csv

2) The data is not tidy - there are 8 columns total, the first four (Initial, Year1, Year2, Year3) belong to the null treatment group and the last four belong to the diet/exercise group. Pivot each set of four columns separately to long format.

3)After pivoting, add a group column to each that labels rows as either "Nll" or "Diet/Exercise", then bind the two together into one data frame

4) Clean up the time variable so the values read as "Initial", "Year 1", "Year 2", "Year 3" in the correct order using a factor

5) calculate the average ulnar calcium measurement at each time point for each group

6)Create a line plot with time on the x-axis and ulnar calcium on the y-axis, showing individual participant lines faintly in the background and a bold group average line on top, colored by treatment group

**Response 2:** Few quick questions before I write anything:

1. What's the exact filename/path you're uploading? Just want to make sure the `read.csv()` call matches exactly.

2. For the individual participant lines in step 6 -- does each row in the original data represent one participant, or is there a participant ID column I should know about?

3. Any preference on the plotting library -- base R or `ggplot2`?

**Prompt 3:** my bad, I forgot to upload the file, ill upload with this prompt

also each row represents one participant, no ID column and use ggplot2

**Response 3:** No worries! I can see the file now. Let me take a quick look at it before writing anything.Hmm, doesn't look like the file came through. Can you try uploading it again? Sometimes it doesn't attach properly.

**Prompt 4:** lol my bad (file attached with this one)

**Response 4:** Got it. I can see the structure clearly. One thing to flag: the header row has duplicate column names (y0, y1, y2, y3, y0, y1, y2, y3) which R will auto-rename to avoid conflicts. I'll handle that in the code. Here's everything:

```r
library(tidyverse)

# Step 1: Read in the data
calcium_raw <- read.csv("calcium.csv")

# Step 2: Pivot each group of 4 columns separately
# R renames duplicate headers to y0, y1, y2, y3, y0.1, y1.1, y2.1, y3.1
null_long <- calcium_raw %>%
  select(y0, y1, y2, y3) %>%
  mutate(id = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -id, names_to = "time", values_to = "ulnar_calcium")

diet_long <- calcium_raw %>%
  select(y0.1, y1.1, y2.1, y3.1) %>%
  mutate(id = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -id, names_to = "time", values_to = "ulnar_calcium")

# Step 3: Add group labels and bind together
null_long <- null_long %>% mutate(group = "Null")
diet_long <- diet_long %>% mutate(group = "Diet/Exercise")
calcium <- bind_rows(null_long, diet_long)

# Step 4: Clean up time variable as an ordered factor
calcium <- calcium %>%
  mutate(time = recode(time,
    "y0"   = "Initial",
    "y0.1" = "Initial",
    "y1"   = "Year 1",
    "y1.1" = "Year 1",
    "y2"   = "Year 2",
    "y2.1" = "Year 2",
    "y3"   = "Year 3",
    "y3.1" = "Year 3"
  )) %>%
  mutate(time = factor(time, levels = c("Initial", "Year 1", "Year 2", "Year 3")))

# Step 5: Calculate group averages per time point
avg_calcium <- calcium %>%
  group_by(group, time) %>%
  summarize(avg_ulnar = mean(ulnar_calcium, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Step 6: Plot
ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = calcium,
    aes(x = time, y = ulnar_calcium, group = interaction(id, group), color = group),
    alpha = 0.2, linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = avg_calcium,
    aes(x = time, y = avg_ulnar, group = group, color = group),
    linewidth = 1.4
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = avg_calcium,
    aes(x = time, y = avg_ulnar, color = group),
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Ulnar Calcium Measurements Over Time by Treatment Group",
    x = "Time Point",
    y = "Ulnar Calcium",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

A couple things worth noting: Since there's no participant ID column, I create one with row_number() before pivoting so ggplot can draw individual lines correctly. The na.rm = TRUE in mean() handles that one participant in the Diet/Exercise group who only has data for some time points (row 17 in the raw file).






# Appendix B: Code Appendix
```{r ref.label=knitr::all_labels()}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
```
